coredata.dc:title: Visible light-induced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline based on Cu/CDs/BiVO₄ ternary core-shell composite: DFT calculations and mechanism

coredata.dc:description: The novel Cu/Carbon dots/BiVO4 ternary core-shell material was prepared by hydrothermal method and applied for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. The CCB-2 composite exhibited the best photocatalytic activity (96.8 %). The ·O2 - and h+ were confirmed the main substances by electron spin response detection. The cycling experiments showed that CCB-2 has outstanding stability and recyclability. Based on calculations, energy band theory and intermediates analysis, the charge transfer pathways in the photocatalytic process were revealed. The photocatalytic mechanism and degradation pathways were elaborated. It was found that the built-in electronic fields were established in the ternary composite and the electrons can easily transfer from inner core Cu to interlayer carbon dots and then to outlayer BiVO4. The Cu/Carbon dots/BiVO4 composite obtained the effective separation of photocatalytic carriers after the irradiation and then showed the excellent performance. This study provided a promising strategy to enhance the application of bismuth-based materials in environmental purification.

head.ce:title: Visible light-induced photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline based on Cu/CDs/BiVO₄ ternary core-shell composite: DFT calculations and mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel Cu/Carbon dots/BiVO4 ternary core-shell material was prepared by hydrothermal method and applied for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. The CCB-2 composite exhibited the best photocatalytic activity (96.8 %). The ·O2 - and h+ were confirmed the main substances by electron spin response detection. The cycling experiments showed that CCB-2 has outstanding stability and recyclability. Based on calculations, energy band theory and intermediates analysis, the charge transfer pathways in the photocatalytic process were revealed. The photocatalytic mechanism and degradation pathways were elaborated. It was found that the built-in electronic fields were established in the ternary composite and the electrons can easily transfer from inner core Cu to interlayer carbon dots and then to outlayer BiVO4. The Cu/Carbon dots/BiVO4 composite obtained the effective separation of photocatalytic carriers after the irradiation and then showed the excellent performance. This study provided a promising strategy to enhance the application of bismuth-based materials in environmental purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is frequently used in the pharmaceutical field for the treatment of various bacterial infectious diseases. It is also used extensively in animal husbandry to promote animal growth and prevent diseases [1]. However, the TC has been found to distribute in surface water, groundwater, soil and natural environments because of the abuse. For example, in nigerian surface water, the concentration of TC can reach 300 ng/L [2,3]. It has the serious threaten to aquatic organisms and even humans through food chain. For example, long-term low-dose exposure may induce antibiotic resistance in the human body, causing ecotoxic effects. According to statistics, over 10,000 tons of tetracycline antibiotics are discharged globally each year [3]. Thus, it is an urgent need to solve the TC pollution in environment.

The photocatalysis is considered to be one of the green and highly promising environmental purification technologies, showing efficient degradation ability and adaptability to complex environments [4–6]. At present, semiconductor photocatalysis technology has been transitioned from laboratory to engineering. The bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is an important semiconductor photocatalyst. It has the forbidden band width about 2.4–2.7 eV, which can respond to irradiation of UV and partial visible light [6,7]. Nevertheless, BiVO4 suffers from a high recombination of photogenerated carriers, which limits the photocatalytic efficiency in practical applications [5]. Some modifications have been carried out to solve the problems, such as metal doping, heterojunction formation and so on [8–10]. Wang et al. prepared ZnFe2O4/BiVO4 heterojunction for ciprofloxacin degradation with the efficiency about 97.0 % [5]. Yang et al. have prepared BiVO4@BiOCl photocatalyst and it was applied in TC removal with the degradation efficiency of more than 90.3 % under visible light [6].

The carbon dots (CDs) have been reported as a novel type of carbon nanomaterial with excellent optical property, good electron transport ability and chemical stability [11]. Moreover, the CDs can act as acceptors of electrons and effectively promote the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus improve the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions [12]. Chen et al. have prepared CQDs/Bi2MoO6 material and achieved the 99.0 % degradation of norfloxacin, in which CDs took effect in the improvement electron hole separation rate [13]. Du et al. have synthesized CNQDs/Bi2MoO6-x composite, which accelerated the separation and migration of photogenic carriers [14]. Therefore, the CDs doping is an effective strategy to overcome the defect of rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers. However, The CDs composite materials still have some shortcomings. For example, the active groups on the surface of CDs are not stable and easy to be destroyed when CDs are exposed outside, thus affect the performance [15].

In order to solve the problem, constructing core-shell structure is an effective protection [16]. A suitable core can not only load CDs uniformly, but also provide as a platform to form built-in electronic field with CDs. There are many materials that can be selected as the core, such as metal oxides and metal nanoparticles (NPs) [17–20]. Among them, the Cu NPs have the unique advantages in stability and electrical conductivity, which can be effectively accelerate the electron transport and improve photocatalytic performance. Until now, Cu/CDs/BiVO4 ternary composite has not be studied in the photocatalysis. In addition, the electron transfer mechanism and the component function in ternary materials should be studied more clearly.

Based on the above research, this work focuses on the preparation and application of Cu/CDs/BiVO4 ternary composite. The composition and microstructure of the catalyst were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and so on. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalyst was evaluated by the removal of TC under simulating sunlight irradiation. The attack sites were identified and the degradation pathways were proposed by using theoretical calculations and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis. The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation was analyzed by free radical trapping experiment and energy band theory. This study provides an efficient solution to the removal of TC in environment. It can promote the further development of photocatalysis technology in the challenges of antibiotic pollution.
The details of chemicals and equipment used in this work are put in Section 1.1 in Supplementary Material (SM).

The synthesis of BiVO4 was modified according to the method of the previous literature [7]. About 60 mL mixed solution containing 970 mg of Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O and 234 mg of NH4VO3 was ultrasonicated for 30 min and subsequently put in a Teflon-lined liner and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. It was washed six times with water and ethanol alternately and desiccated at 60 °C.

The synthesis of CDs was carried out under the improvement of the previous work [11]. Briefly, 1.0507 g of citric acid and 335 μL of ethylenediamine were dissolved into 10 mL of water. They were magnetically stirred continuously for 30 min and set in a reactor and kept at 200 °C for 8 h. After 24 h dialysis (MWCO:1000), the CDs with brown-black powder was obtained.

A mixed solution of 0.02 mM CuCl2·2 H2O, 0.01 mM NaOH and 33 mg·L−1 CDs was poured into 30 mL of pure water and kept in an oil bath at 120°C for 1 h. After the temperature had cooled down, the resulting suspension was washed and centrifugated. Then the Cu/CDs was dispersed in 10 mL water for use.

A certain amount of Cu/CDs suspension was filled into 10 mL water. After ultrasonic processing for 5 min, 0.1 mM ascorbic acid and 0.01 mM CTAB were added to the mixture was marked as solution A. The solution B was obtained by dissolving 5 mM Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O in 2 mL HNO3 and 7.5 mL water. Similarly, 5 mM NH4VO3 was added into 10 mL of 4 mM NaOH solution. Then a certain EDTA was added with stirring, and then the pH was adjusted to 7 to obtain solution C. Finally, the solution A, B and C were evenly blended and stirred for 30 min, then transferred to the reactor and heated to 160 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was cleaned by deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, then desiccated at 60 °C under vacuum for 8 h. For contrast, the CCB-X materials were prepared by adding 1, 2, 3 mL Cu/CDs suspensions in water and named as CCB-1, CCB-2 and CCB-3, respectively. The schematic of synthetic process is shown in Fig. 1.
The details of photocatalytic evaluation used in this work are put in Section 1.2 in SM.
The details of measurements used in this work are put in Section 1.3 in SM.
The details of analysis of TC and intermediates used in this work are put in Section 1.4 in SM.
The details of Density functional theory (DFT) calculation used in this work are put in Section 1.5 in SM.

The XRD was used to examine the crystalline phase structure of different materials. The high crystallinity of materials is displayed in Fig. S1A. The pronounced diffraction peaks of BiVO4 at 18.67°, 28.68°, 30.98° and 47.02° can be attributed to the (101), (112), (004) and (204) crystal planes (PDF#48–0744) [21]. CDs have the main signal of at 24.40°, which reflects the (100) plane and accords to Ref [22]. In the Cu/CDs, the signal from the CDs can be observed. It is worth noting that the peaks of (111) slightly shift towards lower angles with the addition of Cu (Fig. S1b), which is mainly due to doping of Cu leading to a change of the lattice structure in Cu/CDs [23]. The feeble diffraction peak of 24.40° is hardly perceptible in CCB-X composites because the peak intensity of CDs is significantly lower than that of BiVO4 [24]. The diffraction peaks of the BiVO4 in CCB-X composites decrease with the increase of Cu/CDs, which is consistent with the experimental expectation. In partial enlarged pattern of Fig. S1B, the weak diffraction peaks at 43.30° are attributed to the (111) crystal plane of the metal Cu NPs, illustrating that the successful loading of Cu NPs on the material [23,25].

The FT-IR and Raman spectra were used to identify potential interactions between CDs and CCB-2. A few groups are visible on the surface of the CDs, as seen in Fig. S2A. They include the O-H stretching vibration at 3448 cm−1, the C-H bond bending vibration at 2931 cm−1, the C-O strong vibrational absorption at 1652 cm−1, and the N-H bending vibration at 1563 cm−1 [26]. For the BiVO4, the typical peaks at 739 and 472 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of ν3 (VO4 3-) and the stretching vibration of Bi-O, respectively [27,28]. The CCB-X composites also displayed the distinctive peaks of O-H, C-O, V-O, and Bi-O, demonstrating the materials' successful preparation.

The existence of CDs in CCB-2 composite was further identified by Raman spectroscopy. In contrast to BiVO4, CCB-2 exhibits a significant detection of conjugated sp2 carbon atoms in the G-band at 1612 cm−1 and disordered sp3 carbon in the D-band at 1349 cm−1 (Fig. S2B) [29]. Based on the above evidence, it can be proved that CDs is tightly bound in the composite.

The surface morphology of the materials was analyzed by SEM. The Fig. S3A-B shows the BiVO4 is blocky particles with 1–3 μm in size. As shown in Fig. S3C, the CCB-1 appears to be unevenly sphere with some clumps. From Fig. S3D-E, CCB-2 is regularly spherical and uniformly dispersed with the diameter about 1 μm. As can be seen from Fig. S3F, CCB-3 has an irregular bulk structure, which may be related to the high dosage of the precursor solution.

The TEM and HRTEM were used to examine the materials' composition and microstructure. The Fig. 2A clearly shows that the CDs are uniformly dispersed, and the size is about 5 nm spherical carbon particles. The right-top illustration shows the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.208 nm, which consists with the (100) crystal plane in XRD analysis (Fig. S1A). As can be seen from Fig. 2B, the diameter of the Cu core is sphere-liked with the diameter about 200–400 nm. In Fig. 2C-D, the CCB-2 has the evident spherical morphology with the diameter about 1 μm. After the examination carefully, there are Cu cores in the center, which has darker images. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2E-F) show the obvious lattice spacing of BiVO4 (d112=0.308 nm), CDs (d100=0.208 nm) and Cu (d111=0.206 nm). According to the element mapping images in Fig. 2G, the Bi, V, O and C elements are evenly distributed on the material. However, the Cu elements are focused on the cores of the material. The EDS results in Fig. S4 also proves the coexistence of the elements. This confirms the successful preparation of core-shell structures of the composite.

The surface constituent and chemical valence states of CCB-2 were analyzed by XPS. The survey spectrum of BiVO4 ( Fig. 3A) consists of bismuth (Bi), oxygen (O), vanadium (V), and trace amounts of carbon (C) possibly from environment. All elements can be found in the CCB-2. The difference is, there is Cu 2 P peak in CCB-2. The carbon signal is significantly enhanced after adding CDs. In the Fig. 3B, the Bi3+ 4 f7/2 and Bi3+ 4 f5/2 of BiVO4 are located 159.15 and 164.45 eV, respectively. [30]. A minor shift of the Bi peaks in CCB-2 towards the lower binding energy suggests that the Bi-C interaction has altered the Bi chemical environment [31,32]. In Fig. 3C, the high-resolution spectral peaks of O 1 s are 529.78, 530.92 and 532.14 eV, belonging to Bi-O, V-O and surface hydroxyl group (O-H), respectively [33,34]. In Fig. 3D, it can be seen that the binding energies of V5+ 2p3/2 and V5+ 2p1/2 are responsible for the two distinctive peaks of V5+, which are situated at 516.45 and 524.06 eV, respectively [35]. Besides, the XPS spectrum of C 1 s of CCB-2 can be splitted into three peaks. The C-C bond on the graphite-carbon sp2 orbital is located at 284.80 eV (Fig. 3E). The binding energies of the other two peaks at 286.19 and 288.70 eV correspond to C-O-C and CO, respectively [36]. In the Fig. 3F, two peaks located at 932.08 and 951.95 eV were attributed to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 peaks. The other two observable peaks correspond to the satellite peaks of Cu 2p [36]. The above analysis reveals that Cu, CDs and BiVO4 coexist and bind closely in hybrid materials.

The UV–vis spectroscopy can be used to describe the optical characteristics of CDs. The absorption of CDs at different wavelengths can be reflected. As shown in Fig. 4A, there are two characteristic peaks can be observed at 245 and 340 nm. They belong to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 conjugated domains and the n-π* electron leaps dominated by the oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface, respectively [37]. The surface state and size effects of CDs are shown in the right inset, which displays that the CDs solution is light brown under daylight irradiation (left) and blue under 365 nm UV excitation (right) [38]. In order to verify the excellent electron transport capability of the synthesized CDs, the PL emission spectra of CDs were investigated (Fig. 4B, Table S1). The maximum emission wavelength is evidently seen in the 505–560 nm region when the excitation wavelength varies from 440 to 540 nm. It is the electronic transition in the CDs topology with defect state that cause of the continual decrease in fluorescence intensity and the redshift in emission peak. The expansion of sp2 region leads to the shortening of the π-π* band gap, which reduces the electron transfer energy, resulting in a slow redshift of the peak [38]. In fact, the conjugated π structure of CDs can promote the transfer of photogenerated charge under irradiation, and CDs can also receive electrons after photoexcitation [39]. To sum up, the prepared CDs can be used as electron transfer channels to improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

The UV–vis DRS spectroscopy was used to examine the catalyst's light absorption capabilities (Fig. 4C). The absorption edge of BiVO4 is at 550 nm, which indicates that it can absorb ultraviolet light and a small fraction of the visible light. The absorption edges of the composites show an obvious red-shift compared with the monomer, which proves that the introduction of copper and CDs expands the light absorption range. The strong absorption zone of the CCB-X can be observed at the range of 500–750 nm. The band gap energy of BiVO4 and CCB-2 can be determined by Eq. (1) [40]:
(1) αhν = A(hν-Eg) n/2
Where, Eg, A, α and hv are band gap, constant, absorption coefficient and photon energy, respectively. Both BiVO4 and CCB-2 are direct bandgap semiconductors (n = 1) [19]. As can be seen from Fig. 4D, the Eg of BiVO4 and CCB-2 are 2.52 and 2.23 eV, respectively.

The semiconductor's flat band potential (EFB) was determined using the Mott-Schottky curve. AS can be seen from Fig. 4E-F, the Mott-Schottky curve has a positive slope, indicating that the catalyst is an n-type semiconductor. The EFB (vs. Ag/AgCl) for BiVO4 and CCB-2 are −0.84 and −0.95 V, respectively. According to Eq. (2), EFB (vs. Ag/AgCl) can be converted to EFB (vs. NHE) [41]. Thus, the EFB (vs. NHE) of BiVO4 and CCB-2 were −0.64 and −0.75 eV, respectively. Generally speaking, conduction band potential (ECB) vs. NHE is accepted to be about 0.1 eV more negative than EFB vs. NHE [42], so the ECB (vs. NHE) of BiVO4 and CCB-2 are −0.74 and −0.85 eV, respectively. According to Eq. (3), it can be calculated that the valence band (EVB) (vs. NHE) of BiVO4 and CCB-2 is 1.78 and 1.38 eV, respectively.
(2) EFB (vs. NHE) = EFB (vs. Ag/AgCl) + 0.20
(3) EVB (vs. NHE) = ECB (vs. NHE) + Eg (vs. NHE)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band structure and state density analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The CASTEP module of the DFT was used to calculate the total/partial density of state (TDOS/PDOS) and the simulated electronic band structure. Fig. S5A shows the distribution of BiVO4's valence band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM) both located at the same region from X to G, indicating that BiVO4 is a direct bandgap semiconductor with good electronic structure. The electron band structure is studied by state density calculation. The VB and CB composition of BiVO4 can be more fully understood. As shown in Fig. S5B, the valence band electronic state of BiVO4 in the energy range −5.85–0.55 eV is mainly attributable to the strong O 2p state. Meanwhile, the conduction electron states in the energy range of 1.45–8.05 eV are mainly from Bi 6p, O 2p and V3p states. According to the former study, the Bi 6p orbital can speed up the migration rate of photogenerated carriers and decrease the band gap [43].

The electrochemical characteristics of materials were assessed under simulated sunlight. Fig. S6A displays the transient photocurrent change curves. The CCB-2 composite material exhibits the highest photocurrent intensity. It is confirmed that the coupling of BiVO4 and the other two substances can promote the separation of photogenerated carrier.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) Nyquist plot (Fig. S6B) further supports the above results. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) is an important parameter to analyze the EIS Nyquist plot [33]. It is clear that CCB-2 has the smallest Rct value (4.06 Ω), indicating the fastest interfacial electron transfer. According to the thorough analysis, the reassociation of e--h+ slowed down by the addition of Cu and CDs. The CDs acted as charge transfer channels for the materials, thus improving their photocatalytic ability.

After 60 minutes of adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the dark (Fig. S7A), the adsorption efficiency of TC was no more than 12.5 %. Then, the photocatalytic performance of the materials was evaluated under simulated sunlight using TC as the target.

The blank control studies in Fig. 5A demonstrates insignificant TC degradation without the participation of catalyst. The degradation efficiency of TC by BiVO4 is only 66.6 % after 100 min irradiation. After the introduction of CDs or Cu, the degradation effects of CDs/BiVO4 and Cu/BiVO4 were increased to 69.3 % and 92.8 %, respectively. It is reported that the Cu can produce hot electrons after being excited by light, which greatly improves the catalytic ability [43]. Among the composites, the CCB-2 shows the best performance. The TC removal efficiency can reach 96.8 % after 100 min of irradiation. Comparatively, the Cu/CDs/BiVO4 is superior or comparative to the other materials (Table S2). It shows that the CDs plays an important role in the system. The hot electrons produced by Cu are transferred by CDs, which greatly improves the carrier separation efficiency. Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics were examined. The results are displayed in Fig. 5B. The pseudo-first-order kinetics is found in the relationship between TC concentration and irradiation time. CCB-2 has the rate constant (k) of 0.0353 min−1, which exceeds that of BiVO4 by 3.25 times (Table S3).

One of the crucial factors influencing photocatalysis is the dosage of catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5C, the degradation efficiency of TC increases and then decreases as the catalyst was added from 5 to 20 mg (C0 = 10 mg·L−1, pH = 7). In general, additional catalysts can produce more free radicals and active sites, which can accelerate the reaction. However, superfluous material exhibits worse effects. This is due to the shading effect of a large number of particles in the solution. Therefore, the optimal catalyst dosage selected for the subsequent photocatalysis experiments was 15 mg.

The pollutant elimination is impacted by the pH of the solution, which affects the charge and form distribution of the contaminants. As observed from Fig. 5D (C0 = 10 mg·L−1, CCB-2 = 15 mg), the removal efficiency of TC increases first when pH is 3–7, then decreases slightly when pH is 9–11, which is connected with the TC distribution and the material zeta potential From Fig. 5E, the acidity constants (pKa) of TC are 3.3, 7.5, and 9.5, in accordance with pKa1, pKa2, and pKa3, respectively. In acidic environments (pH < 6.85), The TC exists in the form of TC+ when pH< 3.3. Fig. 5F shows the CCB-2 is positively charged when pH is less than the isoelectric point (pHzpc=6.85). There is electrostatic repulsion between CCB-2 and pollutants, which is not conducive to photocatalytic degradation. Similarly, when pH> 7.5, CCB-2 is negatively charged, which has the electrostatic repulsion to the TC- and TC2-. The degradation efficiency of TC decreased in alkaline solution. As a result, pH= 7 was used in the subsequent experiments.

The effect of the pollutant concentration of TC on the reaction is shown in Fig. S7B. The degradation of CCB-2 under simulated sunlight decreased from 96.8 % (10 mg L−1) to 38.2 % (40 mg L−1). The degradation efficiency decreased as the initial concentration of TC increased. This can be attributed to the competition between high TC concentrations and intermediates for active radicals. Therefore, the optimal initial TC concentration selected for the experiment was 10 mg⋅L−1.

The material stability and recyclability were examined by using cyclic experiments. After six cycles, the final rate constants and photodegradation efficiency were kept at 0.022 min−1 and 89.6 %, respectively (Fig. S8A-B). The material before and after the cycling experiments were also analyzed by FT-IR and XRD. By contrast, there are no distinct changes in the signals (Fig. S8C-D). The SEM and TEM of the recycled CCB-2 still have obvious morphologies (Fig. S9A-B). The above results indicate that CCB-2 has excellent degradation performance and can be reused with good stability.

The effect of inorganic ions in the actual water on degradation cannot be ignored. The practical application effect of CCB-2 catalyst was studied by simulating TC solution with different inorganic salt concentration of 0.1 mol/L. As shown in Fig. S10, the degradation efficiency of CCB-2 decreased a little after adding inorganic ions. After adding NaCl, CaCl2, KNO3 and Mg2SO4, respectively, the degradation efficiency remained above 89.2 %.

The total organic carbon (TOC) was used to assess the photocatalyst performance (Fig. S11). The mineralization efficiencies of TC by CCB-2 can reach 29.0 % and 48.4 % in 50 and 100 min under simulated sunlight irradiation, respectively. They are less than the TC removal efficiencies (91.2 % and 96.8 %), which indicates that TC molecules are partially converted to other organic intermediates. It can be expected that more organic pollutants will be transformed to small molecules such as CO2 and H2O after a longer period illumination [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intermediates degradation pathway and DFT calculation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better understand the degradation process of TC, the spectral information of target pollutants and their intermediates was analyzed using Three-dimensional excitation emission matrix technology (3D-EEMs). Due to the multiple electron-withdrawing groups of TC, no obvious fluorescence signals were detected in the TC solution ( Fig. 6A) and the solution after 60 min of dark adsorption (Fig. 6B). However, with the continuation of irradiation, two distinct peaks appear and increase in the fluorescence region of humic and fulvic acid-like substances (Fig. 6C-E) [45,46]. When the irradiation time was 100 min, the intensity of the fluorescence peaks decreases, which illustrates that the TC molecules and the intermediates produced are further decomposed.

The DFT was used to optimize and calculate the molecular structure of TC ( Fig. 7). The Cartesian coordinates (Å) of TC were calculated by DMol3/B3LYP (Table S4). The HOMO is mostly concerned with the methyl carbon, which is vulnerable to assault by electrophilic radicals (h+), as illustrated in Fig. 7A. The LUMO is mainly concentrated in the benzene ring, and easily be attacked by ·O2 - and ·OH. The attack sites can be predicted by using the electrophilic index f
(-) (r) and nucleophilic index f
(+) (r). The nucleophilic/electrophilic properties of the investigated sites were studied based on the dual descriptor of the Fukui function of Eq. (4):
(4)
f
(2) (r) = f
(+) (r)-f
(-) (r)
Where, r means the position vector. At a given position, the more positive f
(2) (r) is, the more vulnerable the "r" site is to be attacked by nucleophilic reagents. Conversely, the more negative f
(2) (r) is, the "r" sites are more vulnerable to be electrophilic attacked. According to Fig. 7B, the C2, C7, C14, C15, C17, C30, C31, O21 and N29, which can be attacked by active species because they have larger absolute values of f
(2) (r) [47].

The reaction intermediates were identified by LC-MS analysis, which further validated the above calculations. The products detected are shown in Fig. S12 and Table S5. By analyzing the intermediates and the Fukui index, three degradation pathways of TC are proposed in Fig. 8.

Pathway I, TC is demethylated and dehydroxylated to form P1 (m/z = 435) under the attack of free radicals. P1 is converted to P2 (m/z = 415) by deamination and methylation. P2 is degraded to p3 (m/z = 318) by ring-opening reaction under the attack of active substances such as h+ and ·O2 -. P4 (m/z = 274) is obtained by the carbonyl-extrusion reaction of P3 [48]. After the further catalysis, p5 (m/z = 114) was obtained.

Pathway II, TC is demethylated to form P6 (m/z = 413). Then it generates P2 and p7 (m/z = 362) through methylation and loss of hydroxyl and amide. The ring-opening reaction of P7 produces P8 (m/z = 349), which is further catalyzed to produce P9 (m/z = 225) and P5 [49].

Pathway III, under the attack of free radicals, TC is transformed to P10 (m/z = 318) by dehydroxylation, deamidation, demethylation, deamination and other reactions. P10 is oxidized by free radicals to form P11 (m/z = 274), which is converted to P12 (m/z = 246) by ring-opening reaction. After the dehydroxylation reaction, P13 (m/z = 186) is formed [47,50]. Eventually, with the prolonged photocatalysis, all intermediates are eventually mineralized to CO2 and H2O.

The different active substances contribute in the TC degradation process. In the capture experiments, ·OH, ·O2 - and h+ were quenched by adding isopropyl alcohol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and EDTA-2Na, respectively (Fig. S13) [51]. The removal efficiency of CCB-2 is 96.8 % when no quencher was added. After IPA was added, TC degradation decreases slightly. It is indicated that the effect of ·OH was relatively small, but not negligible. However, the introduction of EDTA-2Na or BQ greatly reduces the removal efficiency of TC, indicating that h+ and ·O2 -are the primary active species. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments confirm the above results. The details are presented in Section 1.6 of SM. As shown in the Fig. S14, no signal of free radicals was found in the dark. After two minutes’ irradiation, DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH are clearly observed. With the extension of irradiation time to five minutes, the both signals becomes more significant. Therefore, ·O2 - and ·OH are proved the active substances in the experiment.

It is well known that in the process of combining multiple semiconductors, differences in fermi energy levels (Ef) cause the electrons to rearrange and transfer until their fermi levels are in equilibrium. The transfer direction of electrons is always from the higher fermi level to the lower fermi level [52]. In order to explore the properties of the charge transfer and verify the presence of an inherent electric field in CCB-2, DFT calculations were used to analyze the electron migration direction between the three substances. The Cu (111), CDs (002) and BiVO4 (112) were modeled by materials studio (MS). The optimized crystal structures in MS are shown in Fig. 9A-C. The calculations show that their Ef of Cu (111), CDs (002) and BiVO4 (112) are −3.175, −3.374 and −4.276 eV, respectively. Thus, electrons transfer from Cu to CDs to BiVO4. Due to the influence of electron migration during the formation of the composites, potential differences exist between the interfaces, which results in the inherent electric field in the photocatalyst.

The differential charge density can simulate and calculate the electron escape direction between materials, which is one of the powerful tools to verify the existence of built-in electric field [53]. The Cu/CDs and CDs/BiVO4 were modeled by MS program and the optimized structures are shown in Fig. 10A-B by using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) energy functional. The calculated differential charge density is shown in Fig. 10C-D, where blue and yellow blocks represent atoms lose and obtain electrons easily, respectively. It can be seen that the atoms of CDs are mainly yellow in Fig. 10C, which shows that in the Cu/CDs complex, the e- are inclined to flow from Cu to CDs. In Fig. 10D, the atoms of vanadium are mainly yellow, which shows that in the CDs/BiVO4 complex, the e- flow easily from CDs to BiVO4. Thus, the analysis of differential charge density further confirms the existence of a built-in electric field in CCB-2 with electron migration from Cu to CDs, then to BiVO4 (Fig. S15). The results are consistent with the calculation of fermi level above.

To sum up, the photocatalytic mechanism of the CCB-2 composite is schematically shown in Fig. 11. The bandgap of BiVO4 is 2.52 eV. After the introduction of CDs and Cu, the bandgap of CCB-2 is reduced to 2.23 eV, which greatly improves the light utilization. This is corroborated through the optical analysis results in Fig. 4C. The excited electrons can migrate into the LUMO of Cu under simulated sunlight irradiation. In BiVO4, the e- leaps to the ECB, leaving the h+ in the EVB. Due to the action of the built-in electric field, the e- in the LUMO of Cu and the h+ in the EVB of BiVO4 undergo rapid complexation through the CDs channel. A large amount of h+ is left in the HOMO of Cu, which is the main active substance in the degradation process. It is consistent with the results of the capture experiment in Fig. S13. In this process, the e- and h+ preferentially recombine in the carbon point channel. The e- in the ECB of BiVO4 (-0.85 eV vs. NHE) has a strong reducing ability to convert airborne O2 to ·O2 - (−0.33 eV vs. NHE). The EVB of CCB-2 (1.38 eV vs. NHE) is lower than that of 2.27 eV (vs. NHE) (·OH/H2O). Therefore, the compound cannot produce ·OH directly. This suggests that ·OH may originate from the multistep reduction of the conductive O2, rather than being formed through the oxidation of H2O by the h+. As a result, the h+, ·O2 -, and ·OH are able to attack the TC molecules to produce CO2 and H2O. According to the above analysis, the degradation process can be summarized in the following equation:
(5) CCB-2 + hv → e- + h+
(6) e- + O2 → ·O2 –
(7) e- + ·O2 - + 2H+→ H2O2
(8) H2O2 + e-→ ·OH + OH-
(9) TC + h+/·O2 - /·OH → Intermediates + CO2 + H2O

In summary, the Cu/CDs/BiVO4 composite with core-shell structure was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. The structural, morphology characterization and electrochemical properties of CCB-2 nanomaterial were studied. The BiVO4 shell protected the Cu/CDs core and significantly inhibited photo-generated carrier recombination. Under the conditions (CCB-2 dosage: 15 mg; pH: 7; TC concentration: 10 mg/L), the removal efficiency of TC reached 96.8 % and the rate constant was 0.0353 min−1. The repeated experiments show that the removal efficiency maintained at 89.6 % after 6 cycles. The removal efficiency of CCB-2 in the actual wastewater still reached 89.2 %, indicating that it has the potential of engineering application. The electron transfer trend was illustrated by fermi energy level and differential density charge analysis, and the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed in conjunction with energy band theory. Combined with DFT calculations and LC-MS analysis, the labile active sites of TC were confirmed and the degradation pathway was proposed. It was shown that the h+ plays a major role in the degradation reaction and the ·O2 - plays a secondary role through the active species capture experiments and EPR detection. This work opens up a new way to enhance the photoactivity of ternary core-shell material in the degradation of antibiotic.
